vulnerability.title: Set SSH Daemon LogLevel to VERBOSE
vulnerability.description: The VERBOSE parameter configures the SSH daemon to record login and logout activity. To specify the log level in SSH, add or correct the following line in /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf: LogLevel VERBOSE
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then mkdir -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d touch /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf chmod 0600 /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config" LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d"/*.conf if [ -e "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id" "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" else touch "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf" cp "/etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode

triage_decision.reason: Changing the SSH daemon LogLevel to VERBOSE is a standard STIG hardening configuration that does not affect authentication or authorization mechanisms beyond logging. It is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage.

reasoning_messages.reasoning: We need to provide plan then call review_plan. The user wants us to first propose plan. According to instructions in developer instructions. So we must do plan, then review_plan. So first message: describe plan. Then call review_plan with plan_description.

We need to produce plan, call review_plan. But with feedback: "Review output was not valid JSON. Raw: {…]" That implies our plan output earlier was not JSON? We must produce output? The instruction says first response: describe plan. Then call review_plan. The prior attempt probably didn't produce JSON. We need to produce plan string. So we do: plan_description string. Then send review_plan. For now: produce plan:

Plan: modify /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf to set LogLevel VERBOSE; ensure file exists, set permissions 0600; remove any existing LogLevel lines in sshd_config, sshd_config.d/*.conf; maybe use sed -i "/^\s*LogLevel\s\+/Id" ... etc. Then reload sshd.

We'll send plan string.

review_feedback: The plan removes all LogLevel directives, including the one just created, so the desired VERBOSE setting will not actually be applied. The order of operations is critical and the current description would leave the SSH daemon with no LogLevel directive, defeating the remediation goal.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: Review output was not valid JSON. Raw: {"finding_id":"sshd_set_loglevel_verbose","is_optimal":false,"approve":true,"feedback":"The plan correctly patches the LogLevel setting and removes duplicate directives; it will resolve the vulnerabil...

The plan removes all LogLevel directives, including the one just created, so the desired VERBOSE setting will not actually be applied. The order of operations is critical and the current description would leave the SSH daemon with no LogLevel directive, defeating the remediation goal.
concerns: Removal step deletes the LogLevel line that was just added, resulting in no effective LogLevel setting.
The plan does not guarantee a newline at the end of the configuration file, which could cause parsing issues.
Potential risk of misordering operations could result in a locked or misconfigured SSH service.
No explicit check for idempotence; repeated execution may produce unintended changes.
suggested_improvements: Perform the removal of existing LogLevel directives **before** adding the new line to ensure the directive is kept.

Ensure the configuration file ends with a newline, e.g., echo -e 'LogLevel VERBOSE
' > /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d/00-complianceascode-hardening.conf.
Use a temporary file or atomic rename to replace configuration safely and avoid partial writes.
Validate that /etc/ssh/sshd_config.d exists and is writable before attempting to add the file.
Add a check to verify sshd reload succeeded, and consider using 'systemctl try-restart sshd' to avoid lockout during the reload.
